Staged mitral intervention after TAVR was associated with better functional class but numerically lower mortality that did not reach statistical significance.
clinical_implications: Risk stratification based on MR improvement after TAVR can identify high-risk patients who may benefit from staged transcatheter mitral intervention, though further studies are needed to confirm this benefit.

title: International consensus statement on nomenclature and classification of the congenital bicuspid aortic valve and its aortopathy, for clinical, surgical, interventional and research purposes
key_finding: This international consensus establishes standardized nomenclature and classification for bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) recognizing 3 valve types (fused, 2-sinus, partial-fusion) and 3 associated aortopathy types (ascending, root, extended phenotypes).
clinical_implications: This standardized classification system provides universal nomenclature for clinicians, surgeons, and researchers to improve communication and facilitate research in bicuspid aortic valve disease and associated aortopathy.

title: David versus Bentall – a comparison of early and late results after aortic valve-sparing reimplantation and aortic root replacement
key_finding: Both David and Bentall procedures showed excellent early and long-term results with comparable outcomes for early and late mortality, but David procedure had lower incidence of serious bleeding events compared to Bentall procedure.
clinical_implications: The David procedure demonstrates clear advantages over Bentall procedure while avoiding long-term anticoagulation, supporting its expanded use in appropriate candidates with aortic root pathology.

title: Aspirin with or without clopidogrel after transcatheter aortic-valve implantation
key_finding: Among patients undergoing TAVI without indication for anticoagulation, aspirin alone resulted in significantly lower bleeding rates (15.1% vs 26.6%) and fewer composite bleeding/thromboembolic events compared to aspirin plus clopidogrel for 3 months. There was no difference in ischemic events between groups.
clinical_implications: These results support using aspirin monotherapy rather than dual antiplatelet therapy in TAVI patients without anticoagulation indications to reduce bleeding risk without increasing ischemic complications.

title: A Controlled Trial of Rivaroxaban after Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement
key_finding: In patients after TAVR, rivaroxaban 10 mg daily plus aspirin was associated with higher risk of death or thromboembolic events (HR 1.35) and higher bleeding risk compared to antiplatelet therapy alone, leading to early trial termination due to safety concerns.
clinical_implications: This study supports using dual antiplatelet therapy rather than anticoagulation with rivaroxaban for post-TAVR antithrombotic management in patients without established anticoagulation indications.

title: Reduced leaflet motion after transcatheter aortic-valve replacement
key_finding: In the GALILEO-4D substudy, a rivaroxaban-based antithrombotic strategy was more effective than antiplatelet therapy in preventing subclinical leaflet motion abnormalities after TAVR (2.1% vs 10.9% with grade 3+ motion reduction). However, rivaroxaban was associated with higher risks of death, thromboembolic events, and bleeding in the main trial.
clinical_implications: While rivaroxaban reduces subclinical leaflet thrombosis after TAVR, its increased bleeding and thrombotic risks make routine anticoagulation inadvisable for patients without other indications.

title: Five-Year Outcomes with Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement
key_finding: 5-year results from PARTNER 2 show TAVR had 27% higher hazard of death or disabling stroke after 2 years compared to surgical aortic valve replacement. TAVR also showed disadvantages in reoperations and rehospitalizations, raising concerns about durability.
clinical_implications: The 5-year results suggest TAVR may have late disadvantages compared to surgery, particularly regarding long-term durability and effectiveness.

title: Safe Reintroduction of Cardiovascular Services During the COVID-19 Pandemic From the North American Society Leadership
key_finding: This multisocietal consensus document provides recommendations for the safe reintroduction of cardiovascular services, including valve procedures, during the COVID-19 pandemic as regions move beyond peak phases. The document emphasizes balancing essential cardiovascular care while maintaining appropriate safety measures.
clinical_implications: Provides structured guidance for healthcare systems to safely resume valve and other cardiovascular procedures while managing COVID-19 risks and competing resource demands.

title: Restructuring Structural Heart Disease Practice During the COVID-19 Pandemic
key_finding: Presents crisis-driven recommendations for managing structural heart disease patients during COVID-19, focusing on ensuring appropriate treatment while minimizing exposure risk and limiting resource utilization. Reality-based considerations are necessary given the absence of evidence-based data for this unique scenario.
clinical_implications: Provides practical guidance for heart teams to adapt structural heart disease practice during pandemic conditions with limited resources.

title: Early Surgery in Infective Endocarditis: Can it Be Too Early?
key_finding: Pre-operative antibiotic duration has diminishing returns after 7-9 days, with no impact beyond 19-21 days, and staphylococcal and enterococcal infections show high risk of positive valve cultures regardless of antibiotic duration. The greatest impact of antibiotics on culture positivity occurs within the first few days.
clinical_implications: When urgent indications for early valve surgery are present in infective endocarditis, especially with staphylococcal or enterococcal organisms, delaying surgery to administer more antibiotics is unlikely to provide additional patient benefit.

title: Safe Reintroduction of Cardiovascular Services During the COVID-19 Pandemic: From the North American Society Leadership
key_finding: North American cardiovascular societies provide harmonized recommendations for the safe progressive reintroduction of routine cardiovascular procedures and diagnostic tests after the initial peak of the COVID-19 pandemic.
clinical_implications: Provides guidance for prioritizing cardiovascular patients including valve services as healthcare systems return to normal capacity post-COVID surge.

title: 2020 ESC Guidelines for the management of adult congenital heart disease
key_finding: Updated European Society of Cardiology guidelines for the management of adult congenital heart disease, providing comprehensive recommendations for diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up. The guidelines address the growing population of adults with congenital heart disease and their complex cardiovascular needs.
clinical_implications: These guidelines provide standardized, evidence-based recommendations for clinicians managing adults with congenital heart disease, including valve-related complications and interventions in this population.

title: Mitral Valve Replacement With the 15-mm Mechanical Valve: A 20-Year Multicenter Experience
key_finding: Mitral valve replacement with the 15-mm St Jude Medical Masters prosthesis can safely be performed in infants and neonates with median freedom from prosthesis replacement of 3.5 years. Prosthetic valve gradients increased from mean 5.0 mm Hg at discharge to 14.3 mm Hg at 5-year follow-up.
clinical_implications: The 15-mm mechanical valve provides a viable surgical option for MVR in infants when repair is not possible, though replacement is typically needed within 3.5 years due to patient growth.

title: Mechanical Mitral Valve Replacement: A Multicenter Study of Outcomes With Use of 15- to 17-mm Prostheses
key_finding: Among 61 infants receiving 15-17mm mechanical mitral valve replacement, there were 13 in-hospital deaths (21%) and 8 late deaths (17%), with 27 patients (44%) requiring prosthetic valve replacement at median 3.7 years.
clinical_implications: Small mechanical mitral prostheses provide an important salvage option for critically ill neonates and infants when mitral valve repair is not possible, though with significant morbidity and need for eventual replacement due to patient outgrowth.

title: Sizing strategy and implant considerations for the avalus valve
key_finding: Proper sizing technique using the Avalus replica sizer and appropriate surgical technique are critical for optimal hemodynamic performance of the Avalus bioprosthesis in aortic valve replacement. The replica sizer should fit firmly with minimal visible annular tissue.
clinical_implications: Surgeons should use the specific Avalus replica sizer for valve selection and avoid oversizing to optimize hemodynamic outcomes and valve durability.

title: Balloon-Expandable versus Self-Expanding Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement: A Propensity-Matched Comparison from The France-TAVI Registry
key_finding: Self-expanding transcatheter heart valves were associated with higher rates of moderate or greater paravalvular regurgitation (15.5% vs 8.3%) and increased 2-year mortality (29.8% vs 26.6%) compared to balloon-expandable valves in propensity-matched analysis.
clinical_implications: These findings suggest that balloon-expandable and self-expanding TAVR devices may not achieve equivalent clinical outcomes and support the need for randomized trials comparing these technologies head-to-head.

title: A structured approach to native mitral valve infective endocarditis: Is repair better than replacement?
key_finding: A structured approach allowed mitral valve repair in 66% of native endocarditis patients with no survival difference compared to replacement within 1 year, but replacement was associated with decreased late survival beyond 1 year. No differences were found in freedom from recurrent endocarditis or reintervention.
clinical_implications: Early surgery with structured approach enables durable mitral valve repair in most endocarditis cases and may improve long-term outcomes compared to replacement.

title: Risk factors and clinical significance of elevated mitral valve gradient following valve repair for degenerative disease
key_finding: Elevated mitral valve gradients after repair for degenerative disease are associated with patient age, body surface area, ring size, and ring type. Elevated gradients increase risk of mitral valve reintervention but do not affect overall survival or atrial fibrillation rates.
clinical_implications: Surgeons should be aware that elevated postrepair gradients can occur even with true-sized annuloplasty and may lead to higher reintervention rates.

title: Uncertainties and challenges in surgical and transcatheter tricuspid valve therapy: a state-of-the-art expert review
key_finding: Tricuspid regurgitation remains undertreated despite being associated with increased mortality, with surgery being the only Class I guideline-recommended therapy, though many aspects of TR including optimal timing and treatment selection remain poorly understood. Transcatheter tricuspid therapies are under development but face significant knowledge gaps.
clinical_implications: This review highlights the need for better understanding of TR disease processes, improved risk stratification, and development of evidence-based guidelines for optimal timing and technique selection in tricuspid valve interventions.

title: Isolated tricuspid valve surgery: impact of aetiology and clinical presentation on outcomes
key_finding: Isolated tricuspid valve surgery was associated with high mortality and morbidity (10% in-hospital mortality, 75% 5-year survival), predicted by severity of clinical presentation but not by TR mechanism.
clinical_implications: Results suggest that tricuspid valve intervention should be performed earlier in the disease course before severe symptoms develop.

title: Clonal haematopoiesis in patients with degenerative aortic valve stenosis undergoing transcatheter aortic valve implantation
key_finding: Acquired somatic mutations in CHIP-driver genes DNMT3A and TET2 occur frequently in patients with severe degenerative aortic stenosis (33.3% prevalence) and are associated with increased pro-inflammatory leucocyte subsets and profoundly increased medium-term all-cause mortality following successful TAVI.
clinical_implications: Genetic screening for CHIP mutations may help identify high-risk patients undergoing TAVI who require enhanced monitoring and potentially targeted anti-inflammatory therapies.

title: Leaflet immobility and thrombosis in transcatheter aortic valve replacement
key_finding: Subclinical transcatheter heart valve thrombosis occurs more frequently than previously recognized after TAVR, is better detected by CT than echocardiography, and occurs less frequently with anticoagulation compared to dual antiplatelet therapy.
clinical_implications: Routine CT surveillance may be needed to detect subclinical valve thrombosis after TAVR, and anticoagulation strategies may need reconsideration for optimal valve function.

title: Impact of myocardial fibrosis on left ventricular remodelling, recovery, and outcome after transcatheter aortic valve implantation in different haemodynamic subtypes of severe aortic stenosis
key_finding: Myocardial fibrosis (>11%) was associated with delayed LV recovery after TAVI and independently predicted cardiovascular mortality (26.5% vs 2%, HR 27.4), with highest fibrosis levels in low EF, low-gradient AS patients.
clinical_implications: Histological myocardial fibrosis assessment may help identify TAVI patients at higher risk for cardiovascular death and delayed recovery.

title: Age over 35 years is associated with increased mortality after pulmonary valve replacement in repaired tetralogy of Fallot: results from the UK National Congenital Heart Disease Audit database
key_finding: Patients over 35 years of age with repaired tetralogy of Fallot have a 5.6-fold increased risk of death at 10 years after pulmonary valve replacement compared to those under 35 years (10.4% vs 1.3%). Age 35 years was identified as the optimal cut-off for late mortality risk.
clinical_implications: The findings suggest that earlier timing of pulmonary valve replacement before age 35 may improve outcomes in repaired tetralogy of Fallot patients.

title: Transcatheter Mitral Valve Replacement Guided by Echocardiographic-CT Scan Fusion Early Human Clinical Experience
key_finding: Echo-CT fusion imaging provides comprehensive peri-procedural guidance for transcatheter mitral valve replacement by combining pre-operative CT with live 3D TEE for improved visualization and navigation. The technique is particularly beneficial for catheter crossing of the annular plane, positioning check of delivery systems, and assessment of LVOT impact after prosthesis deployment.
clinical_implications: Echo-CT fusion represents a promising tool that may improve procedural guidance and outcomes for transcatheter mitral valve replacement procedures.

title: Extracellular Myocardial Volume in Patients With Aortic Stenosis
key_finding: In patients with severe aortic stenosis, increased extracellular volume fraction (ECV%) correlates with markers of left ventricular decompensation and is independently associated with all-cause mortality after aortic valve intervention with a hazard ratio of 1.10 per percent increase.
clinical_implications: ECV% measured by CMR can serve as a powerful prognostic tool to identify patients with aortic stenosis at higher risk of mortality following valve intervention.

title: Role of Teleproctoring in Challenging and Innovative Structural Interventions Amid the COVID-19 Pandemic and Beyond
key_finding: Teleproctoring technology can be successfully used to perform complex structural heart interventions including reverse LAMPOON-assisted transcatheter mitral valve replacement when travel restrictions prevent on-site proctorship.
clinical_implications: Teleproctoring enables continuation of complex valve interventions during pandemic travel restrictions and has potential for facilitating knowledge dissemination beyond the current crisis.

title: Prognostic Value of Left Ventricular Global Longitudinal Strain in Patients With Secondary Mitral Regurgitation
key_finding: In patients with secondary mitral regurgitation, impaired LV global longitudinal strain (<7.0%) was independently associated with increased mortality risk, whereas LVEF was not predictive. Patients with impaired LV GLS had significantly higher mortality rates at 1-, 2-, and 5-year follow-up compared to those with preserved function.
clinical_implications: LV global longitudinal strain may be more useful than ejection fraction for risk stratification and patient selection in secondary mitral regurgitation, particularly when considering MitraClip therapy.

title: Managing Severe Aortic Stenosis in the COVID-19 Era
key_finding: The paper presents strategies for managing patients with severe aortic stenosis during COVID-19, recommending risk stratification based on symptom severity and transitions to virtual assessments to minimize patient exposure. The authors suggest deferring aortic valve replacement referrals for mild, stable symptoms until the pandemic abates while balancing the risks of delayed treatment against COVID-19 exposure in high-risk patients.
clinical_implications: Clinicians should stratify severe AS patients by symptom severity and utilize telemedicine to minimize COVID-19 exposure while carefully weighing the risks of delayed valve replacement against infection risk.

title: The impact of uncorrected mild aortic insufficiency at the time of left ventricular assist device implantation
key_finding: Patients with uncorrected mild aortic insufficiency had higher risk of progression to moderate or greater aortic insufficiency after LVAD implantation with worse functional status and higher incidence of heart failure-related readmissions (hazard ratio 2.62) compared to patients without aortic insufficiency.
clinical_implications: These findings support consideration of concomitant aortic valve procedures for mild aortic insufficiency at the time of LVAD implantation to improve patient quality of life.

title: Transcatheter Mitral-Valve Repair in Patients with Heart Failure
key_finding: Letters to the editor discuss contradictory evidence between MITRA-FR and COAPT trials regarding efficacy of transcatheter mitral valve repair for functional mitral regurgitation, with concerns raised about data inconsistencies and patient selection criteria.
clinical_implications: The contradictory trial results highlight the need for better patient selection criteria and standardized imaging guidance for transcatheter mitral valve repair in secondary mitral regurgitation.

title: Long-Term Implications of Atrial Fibrillation in Patients With Degenerative Mitral Regurgitation
key_finding: In patients with degenerative mitral regurgitation, atrial fibrillation (both paroxysmal and persistent) is independently associated with excess long-term mortality. Surgery improves survival in all rhythm subsets, but AF continues to confer excess risk post-surgery.
clinical_implications: Detection of atrial fibrillation, even paroxysmal, should trigger prompt consideration for mitral valve surgery in patients with degenerative mitral regurgitation.

title: Long-Term Mortality and Early Valve Dysfunction According to Anticoagulation Use: The FRANCE-TAVI registry
key_finding: Anticoagulation at discharge was independently associated with lower rates of bioprosthetic valve dysfunction (adj. OR 0.54) while neither aspirin nor clopidogrel were independently associated with mortality after TAVR.
clinical_implications: Anticoagulation may provide benefit in preventing early valve dysfunction after TAVR beyond traditional antiplatelet therapy approaches.

title: Intermediate-term outcome of 500 consecutive rapid-deployment surgical aortic valve procedures
key_finding: The Edwards INTUITY rapid-deployment aortic valve showed excellent intermediate-term results with 30-day mortality of 0.8%, overall survival of 94% at 1 year and 89% at 3 years, with mean gradients remaining low (11-13 mmHg) throughout follow-up. New pacemaker implantation was required in 8.6% of patients, with only one case of structural degeneration at 6 years.
clinical_implications: Rapid-deployment aortic valves provide excellent hemodynamic performance and safety with reduced operative times, facilitating minimally invasive approaches, though pacemaker rates remain a concern.

title: Reinterventions after freestyle stentless aortic valve replacement: an assessment of procedural risks
key_finding: Repeat aortic valve interventions after stentless AVR carry an increased but acceptable early mortality risk of 9.3%, with mortality decreasing to zero in the past decade for elective cases. Transcatheter valve-in-valve procedures had higher mortality (25%) due to coronary obstruction complications.
clinical_implications: Reintervention after stentless AVR has acceptable risk when performed electively, but transcatheter valve-in-valve requires careful assessment of coronary artery obstruction risk.

title: The role of transcatheter mitral valve therapy in heart failure
key_finding: Mitral valve regurgitation is detected in up to 75% of patients with heart failure, and interventional mitral valve therapies like MitraClip can treat mitral regurgitation with very low morbidity and mortality rates. However, the therapeutic benefit and role in patients with secondary mitral regurgitation due to heart failure remains to be investigated.
clinical_implications: Transcatheter mitral valve interventions offer minimally invasive treatment options for high-risk heart failure patients with mitral regurgitation, though optimal patient selection and timing require further study.

title: Infective endocarditis after transcatheter aortic valve implantation: a nationwide study
key_finding: The risk for prosthetic valve endocarditis after TAVI was 1.4% in the first year and 0.8% per year thereafter, with 1-year survival after PVE diagnosis of 58% and 5-year survival of 29%.
clinical_implications: The incidence of PVE after TAVI is similar to surgical bioprostheses, making TAVI a safe option for valve replacement in appropriate patients.

title: Postimplant biological aortic prosthesis degeneration: challenges in transcatheter valve implants
key_finding: TAVI valves may exhibit structural valve degeneration due to inherent challenges of deployment mode similar to surgical biological valves which typically degenerate after 10-15 years. The impact on postimplant degeneration of TAVI valves compared to SAVR needs further understanding.
clinical_implications: Expanding TAVI indications should consider potential shortcomings learned from 60 years of SAVR experience, particularly regarding long-term durability and structural valve degeneration.

title: Minimally invasive mitral valve repair for functional mitral regurgitation
key_finding: Modern mitral valve surgery combining 3D endoscopic minimally invasive approach with standardized subannular repair shows improved perioperative results and long-term stability compared to isolated annuloplasty for functional mitral regurgitation. The approach addresses pathophysiological background of type IIIb FMR rather than just annular reduction.
clinical_implications: This comprehensive surgical approach may offer better outcomes than traditional isolated annuloplasty or valve replacement strategies for functional mitral regurgitation patients.

title: Outcomes of Veterans Undergoing TAVR Within Veterans Affairs Medical Centers: Insights From the Veterans Affairs Clinical Assessment, Reporting, and Tracking Program
key_finding: 959 veterans underwent TAVR at 8 VA centers with 30-day mortality of 2.9% and 1-year mortality of 14.0%, with outcomes comparing favorably to benchmark data outside the VA system.
clinical_implications: Veterans undergoing TAVR at VA centers achieve clinical outcomes comparable to non-VA centers despite significant comorbidities in this predominantly elderly male population.

title: 1-Year Outcomes of the CENTERA-EU Trial Assessing a Novel Self-Expanding Transcatheter Heart Valve
key_finding: The CENTERA self-expanding transcatheter heart valve demonstrated mid-term safety and effectiveness at 1 year with 9.1% overall mortality, 4.6% cardiovascular mortality, stable hemodynamics with mean gradient 8.1 mmHg, and low permanent pacemaker rate of 6.5%.
clinical_implications: The CENTERA THV shows promising 1-year outcomes in high-risk patients with aortic stenosis, with low mortality and pacemaker rates supporting its use in this population.

title: Myocardial Scar and Mortality in Severe Aortic Stenosis: Data From the BSCMR Valve Consortium
key_finding: Myocardial scar detected by cardiovascular magnetic resonance was independently associated with 2-fold higher all-cause mortality in patients with severe aortic stenosis undergoing valve intervention. Every 1% increase in left ventricular scar burden was associated with 11% higher mortality risk.
clinical_implications: Preoperative CMR assessment of myocardial scar could help optimize timing of aortic valve intervention before irreversible myocardial damage occurs.

title: Outcome after transvascular transcatheter aortic valve implantation in 2016
key_finding: In 2016, in-hospital mortality after transvascular TAVI was 2.6%, numerically lower than isolated SAVR (2.9%) for the first time. TAVI showed significantly lower mortality than SAVR in very high-risk (11.3% vs 23.6%), high-risk (4.1% vs 9.2%), and intermediate-risk patients (3.0% vs 4.6%).
clinical_implications: These results support TAVI as standard of care across risk categories and contribute to redefining treatment recommendations for aortic stenosis.

title: Predictors of survival in octogenarians after mitral valve surgery for degenerative disease: The Mitral Surgery in Octogenarians study
key_finding: In octogenarians with degenerative mitral valve disease, mitral valve repair had lower 30-day mortality compared to replacement (4.7% vs 18.6%) and provided better long-term survival.
clinical_implications: Mitral valve repair should be preferred over replacement in fit octogenarians with degenerative mitral valve disease when feasible.

title: A 20-year multicenter analysis of dialysis-dependent patients who had aortic or mitral valve replacement: Implications for valve selection
key_finding: Dialysis-dependent patients undergoing valve replacement had poor long-term survival with 5-year survival of 23% for biological valves and 33% for mechanical valves, with no significant survival difference between valve types after multivariable adjustment.
clinical_implications: Biological valves are suitable for most dialysis patients given poor overall survival, except for young patients without diabetes or severe heart failure symptoms who may benefit from mechanical valves.

title: Mini-Stern Trial: A randomised trial comparing mini-sternotomy to full median sternotomy for aortic valve replacement
key_finding: Compared to conventional median sternotomy approach for surgical AVR, mini-sternotomy did not hasten recovery or hospital discharge and was not cost-effective in the UK NHS setting.
clinical_implications: This randomized trial suggests that mini-sternotomy may not provide the perceived advantages of faster recovery or cost savings compared to full sternotomy for aortic valve replacement.

title: Surgical or Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement
key_finding: Commentary on SURTAVI trial discussing concerns about cognitive decline as an outcome not considered in TAVR vs surgery comparisons, and questions about valve-patient mismatch and hemodynamic performance differences between treatment groups.
clinical_implications: Highlights the need for prospective studies evaluating cognitive decline after TAVR in intermediate-risk patients and consideration of minimally invasive surgical approaches as alternatives to conventional surgery.

title: Outcomes in Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement for Bicuspid Versus Tricuspid Aortic Valve Stenosis
key_finding: TAVR in bicuspid aortic stenosis had similar 2-year mortality compared to tricuspid stenosis (17.2% vs 19.4%) but lower device success rate (85.3% vs 91.4%). Procedural differences were observed with early-generation devices but not with new-generation devices.
clinical_implications: TAVR can be safely performed in bicuspid aortic stenosis with comparable long-term outcomes to tricuspid disease, though new-generation devices should be preferred for better procedural success.

title: Transcatheter Mitral Valve Replacement for Degenerated Bioprosthetic Valves and Failed Annuloplasty Rings
key_finding: TMVR for failed annuloplasty rings (valve-in-ring) had lower technical and device success rates and higher 1-year mortality (28.7% vs 12.6%) compared to valve-in-valve procedures for degenerated bioprostheses.
clinical_implications: Mitral valve-in-ring procedures carry higher risk of complications and mortality compared to valve-in-valve procedures, requiring careful patient selection and procedural planning.

title: Impact of Changes in Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction on Survival After Transapical Aortic Valve Implantation
key_finding: LVEF worsened in a small number of patients after transapical TAVI, but this change was not associated with worse postoperative outcomes. Patients with low LVEF showed better improvement, and progressive reduction of sheath diameter does not significantly affect LVEF changes.
clinical_implications: Changes in LVEF after transapical TAVI do not predict survival or clinical outcomes, providing reassurance about the safety of the transapical approach.

title: Transcatheter aortic valve implantation using the ACURATE TA™ system: 1-year outcomes and comparison of 500 patients from the SAVI registries
key_finding: The ACURATE TA system showed low complication rates with 30-day and 1-year mortality of 6.8% and 19.9%, stroke rates of 2.2% and 3.7%, and paravalvular leakage ≥2+ in only 2.6% at 1 year. Outcomes were similar between the SAVI-1 and SAVI-2 registries.
clinical_implications: Transapical TAVR with the ACURATE TA device demonstrates safety and efficacy in real-world all-comers patients with stable performance outcomes through 1 year.

title: Safety, effectiveness and haemodynamic performance of a new stented aortic valve bioprosthesis
key_finding: The Avalus aortic valve bioprosthesis demonstrated good safety profile and clinical effectiveness with 96.4% survival at 1 year, though bleeding rates were higher than acceptable thresholds. Linearized rates were below acceptable levels for death, valve-related thromboembolism, valve thrombosis, paravalvular leak, and endocarditis.
clinical_implications: The Avalus valve shows promise as a surgical aortic valve replacement option with good short-term outcomes, though elevated bleeding rates warrant attention and may be related to anticoagulation practices.

title: 2017 ESC/EACTS Guidelines for the management of valvular heart disease
key_finding: The 2017 ESC/EACTS Guidelines provide comprehensive recommendations for the management of all forms of valvular heart disease, including aortic stenosis, aortic regurgitation, mitral stenosis, mitral regurgitation, and tricuspid valve disease. The guidelines incorporate new evidence on percutaneous valve interventions alongside traditional surgical approaches.
clinical_implications: These guidelines establish the European standard of care for valvular heart disease management, influencing clinical decision-making across all valve interventions and surgical procedures.

title: Standardized definitions of structural deterioration and valve failure in assessing long-term durability of transcatheter and surgical aortic bioprosthetic valves: a consensus statement from the European Association of Percutaneous Cardiovascular Interventions (EAPCI) endorsed by the European Society of Cardiology (ESC) and the European Association for Cardio-Thoracic Surgery (EACTS)
key_finding: This consensus statement establishes standardized definitions for structural valve deterioration and valve failure to enable consistent assessment of long-term durability in both transcatheter and surgical aortic bioprosthetic valves. The document addresses the need for uniform criteria to evaluate valve dysfunction beyond 5 years after implantation.
clinical_implications: These standardized definitions will enable more consistent and comparable research on valve durability, facilitating better understanding of long-term outcomes for TAVI and SAVR procedures.

title: Standardized definitions of structural deterioration and valve failure in assessing long-term durability of transcatheter and surgical aortic bioprosthetic valves: a consensus statement from the European Association of Percutaneous Cardiovascular Interventions (EAPCI) endorsed by the European Society of Cardiology (ESC) and the European Association for Cardio-Thoracic Surgery (EACTS)
key_finding: This consensus statement establishes standardized definitions for structural valve deterioration (SVD) and valve failure to enable consistent assessment of long-term durability of both transcatheter and surgical aortic bioprosthetic valves. The definitions encompass biological, pathological and hemodynamic mechanisms using contemporary non-invasive imaging.
clinical_implications: These standardized definitions will enable more consistent and comparable reporting of valve durability outcomes across studies of TAVI and SAVR, facilitating better assessment of long-term valve performance.

title: Staging classification of aortic stenosis based on the extent of cardiac damage
key_finding: A new staging classification for aortic stenosis based on extent of extravalvular cardiac damage (Stages 0-4) demonstrated that higher stages were independently associated with increased one-year mortality after aortic valve replacement (HR 1.46 per stage increment, P<0.0001). One-year mortality increased progressively from 4.4% (Stage 0) to 24.5% (Stage 4).
clinical_implications: This staging system provides an objective method to risk stratify patients with severe aortic stenosis beyond traditional symptom and comorbidity assessment, potentially improving patient selection and counseling for aortic valve replacement.

title: Final 5-year clinical and echocardiographic results for treatment of severe aortic stenosis with a self-expanding bioprosthesis from the ADVANCE Study
key_finding: Five-year results in real-world, high-risk patients undergoing TAVI with the self-expanding CoreValve showed all-cause mortality of 50.7%, major stroke rate of 5.4%, with stable hemodynamics and low rates of valve dysfunction (2.6%) and reinterventions (1.2%). Moderate aortic regurgitation decreased from 12.8% to 8.0% over time.
clinical_implications: These results provide evidence for continued valve durability and low reintervention rates at 5 years in real-world elderly, high-risk patients treated with self-expanding TAVI.

title: 1-year outcomes post-transcatheter aortic valve implantation using the latest generation of the balloon-expandable transcatheter heart valve
key_finding: In the SOURCE 3 registry of 1946 patients, the SAPIEN 3 valve demonstrated low 1-year mortality (12.6%), cardiovascular mortality (8.0%), stroke rate (3.1%), and rare severe paravalvular leakage (zero severe, 2.6% moderate).
clinical_implications: The latest generation SAPIEN 3 valve provides excellent 1-year outcomes with minimal paravalvular leakage, supporting its use across broader patient populations including intermediate-risk patients.

title: Possible Subclinical Leaflet Thrombosis in Bioprosthetic Aortic Valves
key_finding: Multiple correspondence letters discussing subclinical leaflet thrombosis in bioprosthetic aortic valves after TAVR, with reported incidence ranging from 7% to 40% and concerns about the relationship to neurologic events.
clinical_implications: The findings raise questions about optimal antithrombotic therapy after TAVR but the link to clinical events remains unclear and premature changes to practice are cautioned.

title: Possible Subclinical Leaflet Thrombosis in Bioprosthetic Aortic Valves
key_finding: Multiple correspondence letters discuss subclinical leaflet thrombosis in bioprosthetic aortic valves after TAVR, with varying incidence rates (7% vs 40%) and potential mechanisms including altered flow patterns in the sinuses of Valsalva.
clinical_implications: The findings raise questions about anticoagulation strategies after TAVR but authors caution against premature changes to clinical practice given weak links to clinical events.

title: Possible Subclinical Leaflet Thrombosis in Bioprosthetic Aortic Valves
key_finding: Multiple correspondence letters discussing subclinical leaflet thrombosis in bioprosthetic aortic valves after TAVR, with reported incidence ranging from 7% to 40% depending on valve type and antithrombotic strategy.
clinical_implications: The findings raise questions about optimal anticoagulation strategies after TAVR and suggest that valve design and hemodynamic factors may influence thrombosis risk.

title: Possible Subclinical Leaflet Thrombosis in Bioprosthetic Aortic Valves
key_finding: Multiple letters to the editor discussing subclinical leaflet thrombosis in bioprosthetic aortic valves after TAVR, with varying reported incidence rates and potential mechanisms including altered flow patterns in sinuses of Valsalva.
clinical_implications: Highlights the need for careful consideration of anticoagulation strategies in TAVR patients and warrants further study before major changes to clinical practice.

title: Evolut R Implantation to Treat Severe Pure Aortic Regurgitation in a Patient With Mitral Bioprosthesis
key_finding: Successful implantation of Evolut R transcatheter aortic valve in an 82-year-old patient with severe pure aortic regurgitation and prior mitral valve replacement, resulting in trivial paravalvular regurgitation and improved LV function.
clinical_implications: Demonstrates feasibility of TAVI in complex cases with pure aortic regurgitation and prior mitral valve surgery despite these typically being exclusion criteria.

title: Safety and feasibility of a novel adjustable mitral annuloplasty ring: a multicentre European experience
key_finding: The adjustable mitral annuloplasty ring had 1% operative mortality and 93% 1-year survival, but ring adjustment was attempted in only 12 patients with mixed success and 5 patients required reoperation after 11±9 months.
clinical_implications: This adjustable ring device may provide an additional treatment option for functional mitral regurgitation patients at risk for recurrent MR, but patient selection appears crucial and further trials are needed.

title: One-year outcomes and predictors of mortality after MitraClip therapy in contemporary clinical practice: results from the German transcatheter mitral valve interventions registry
key_finding: The 1-year mortality after MitraClip therapy was 20.3%, with 63.3% of patients achieving NYHA class I or II functional status at 1 year compared to 11.0% at baseline. Procedural failure had the highest hazard ratio (4.36) for predicting 1-year mortality.
clinical_implications: MitraClip therapy provides significant clinical improvement in high-risk patients with mitral regurgitation, but procedural success is the most critical factor for 1-year survival outcomes.

title: The Incidence and Predictors of Early and Mid-term Clinically Relevant Neurological Events after Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Real-World Patients: The CoreValve ADVANCE Study
key_finding: Overall stroke rate was 1.4% through the first day post-TAVR, 3.0% at 30 days, and 5.6% at 2 years, with different predictors of neurological events based on timing after procedure.
clinical_implications: TAVR with the CoreValve system demonstrates low stroke rates in real-world patients, with time-specific risk factors that can guide patient selection and monitoring strategies.

title: Clinical Trial Design Principles and Endpoint Definitions for Transcatheter Mitral Valve Repair and Replacement: Part 1: Clinical Trial Design Principles A Consensus Document From the Mitral Valve Academic Research Consortium
key_finding: MVARC established consensus recommendations for clinical trial design principles to evaluate transcatheter and surgical therapies for mitral regurgitation, addressing study population selection, endpoints, and statistical considerations. The document provides standardized approaches to improve consistency and interpretability of mitral valve therapy clinical studies.
clinical_implications: These consensus guidelines provide a framework for designing robust clinical trials for mitral valve interventions and enable standardized comparison of outcomes across different devices and approaches.

title: Clinical Outcomes and Revascularization Strategies in Patients With Low-Flow, Low-Gradient Severe Aortic Valve Stenosis According to the Assigned Treatment Modality
key_finding: Among patients with low ejection fraction, low-gradient severe aortic stenosis, both SAVR and TAVR improved survival compared to medical therapy, with incomplete revascularization predicting worse cardiovascular outcomes.
clinical_implications: LEF-LG severe AS patients benefit from valve intervention over medical therapy, and complete revascularization should be considered when feasible to optimize outcomes.

title: Transcatheter aortic valve implantation in failed bioprosthetic surgical valves
key_finding: In patients undergoing valve-in-valve TAVI for degenerated bioprosthetic aortic valves, overall 1-year survival was 83.2%, with worse survival in patients with small surgical bioprostheses (≤21 mm) and those with predominant stenosis.
clinical_implications: Small bioprostheses and baseline stenosis are risk factors for mortality after valve-in-valve procedures, suggesting these patients may need careful risk-benefit assessment.

title: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation in Failed Bioprosthetic Surgical Valves
key_finding: Transcatheter valve-in-valve implantation for failed bioprosthetic aortic valves achieved 83.2% one-year survival, with lower survival in patients with small surgical bioprostheses (74.8%) and those with predominant stenosis (76.6%).
clinical_implications: Valve-in-valve implantation is feasible for failed bioprosthetic valves but outcomes vary based on surgical valve size and failure mode.

title: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Bicuspid Aortic Valve Disease
key_finding: TAVR in bicuspid aortic valves is feasible with 89.9% device success and 17.5% one-year mortality, but high incidence of post-implantation AR (28.4%) occurs, which is reduced to 17.4% when MSCT-based sizing is used.
clinical_implications: MSCT-based TAV sizing should be strongly considered in bicuspid aortic valve patients undergoing TAVR to reduce paravalvular regurgitation.

title: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Bicuspid Aortic Valve Disease
key_finding: TAVR in bicuspid aortic valve disease is feasible with encouraging short- and intermediate-term outcomes, but high post-implantation aortic regurgitation (28.4%) occurs, which is reduced to 17.4% when CT-based sizing is used.
clinical_implications: CT-based valve sizing should be used in bicuspid aortic valve TAVR to reduce paravalvular regurgitation and improve procedural outcomes.

title: Treatment of aortic stenosis with a self-expanding transcatheter valve: the International Multi-centre ADVANCE Study
key_finding: The CoreValve self-expanding transcatheter aortic valve system demonstrated low 30-day mortality (4.5%) and stroke rates (3.0%) in higher-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis. One-year all-cause mortality was 17.9% with outcomes stratified by EuroSCORE risk.
clinical_implications: The study supports TAVI with the CoreValve system as a safe and effective treatment option for higher-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis in real-world practice.

title: Treatment of aortic stenosis with a self-expanding transcatheter valve: the International Multi-centre ADVANCE Study
key_finding: The CoreValve System demonstrated safety and effectiveness in higher risk real-world patients with severe aortic stenosis, with 30-day all-cause mortality of 4.5% and 12-month all-cause mortality of 17.9%.
clinical_implications: This study supports TAVI with the CoreValve system as a viable treatment option for high-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis in real-world clinical practice.

title: Increased risk of acute kidney injury in patients undergoing tricuspid valve surgery
key_finding: Tricuspid valve surgery carries a high 30% incidence of postoperative acute kidney injury (AKI) defined by RIFLE criteria, with AKI severity strongly associated with increased mortality (OR 4.2 for each RIFLE class increase) and prolonged intubation.
clinical_implications: The high risk of AKI after tricuspid valve surgery should be considered in perioperative planning and patient counseling, with particular attention to identified risk factors including older age, male gender, previous surgery, preoperative anemia, longer cardiopulmonary bypass time, and valve replacement versus repair.

title: Two-Year Outcomes after Transcatheter or Surgical Aortic-Valve Replacement
key_finding: At 2-year follow-up, TAVR and surgical aortic valve replacement had similar mortality rates (33.9% vs 35.0%), but paravalvular regurgitation was more frequent after TAVR and associated with increased late mortality.
clinical_implications: TAVR is supported as an alternative to surgery in high-risk patients, though paravalvular regurgitation remains a concern requiring monitoring.

title: Two-Year Outcomes after Transcatheter or Surgical Aortic-Valve Replacement
key_finding: At 2-year follow-up, TAVR and surgical aortic valve replacement had similar mortality rates (33.9% vs 35.0%) in high-risk patients, but TAVR was associated with more frequent paravalvular regurgitation which correlated with increased late mortality.
clinical_implications: TAVR is supported as an alternative to surgery in high-risk patients, but paravalvular regurgitation remains a concern requiring careful monitoring.

title: Transcatheter versus Surgical Aortic-Valve Replacement in High-Risk Patients
key_finding: In high-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, transcatheter and surgical aortic valve replacement showed similar 1-year mortality rates (24.2% vs 26.8%), meeting non-inferiority criteria. TAVR had higher rates of major stroke and vascular complications, while surgical replacement had higher rates of bleeding and atrial fibrillation.
clinical_implications: TAVR represents a viable alternative to surgical valve replacement in high-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, with similar survival outcomes but different complication profiles.

title: Transcatheter versus Surgical Aortic-Valve Replacement in High-Risk Patients
key_finding: In high-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, transcatheter aortic-valve replacement (TAVR) showed non-inferiority to surgical replacement at 1 year (24.2% vs 26.8% mortality, P=0.44), with similar survival rates but different periprocedural risk profiles.
clinical_implications: TAVR provides a viable alternative to surgical aortic valve replacement in high-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, offering similar survival outcomes with different risk-benefit profiles.

title: Heterotopic transcatheter tricuspid valve implantation: first-in-man application of a novel approach to tricuspid regurgitation
key_finding: First-in-human case of transcatheter heterotopic tricuspid valve implantation in the inferior vena cava resulted in immediate abolition of venous regurgitation and clinical improvement at 8-week follow-up. The custom-made self-expanding valve was successfully implanted percutaneously in a 79-year-old patient with severe tricuspid regurgitation deemed inoperable.
clinical_implications: This novel heterotopic approach may provide a therapeutic option for inoperable patients with severe tricuspid regurgitation and venous congestion.

title: Standardized endpoint definitions for transcatheter aortic valve implantation clinical trials: a consensus report from the Valve Academic Research Consortium
key_finding: The Valve Academic Research Consortium developed standardized consensus endpoint definitions for transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) clinical trials including criteria for mortality, stroke, bleeding, vascular complications, and prosthetic valve performance. These standardized definitions aim to improve research quality and enable meaningful comparisons between TAVI clinical trials.
clinical_implications: These consensus endpoint definitions provide a framework for consistent data collection and reporting across TAVI studies, facilitating better evaluation of this catheter-based therapy for regulatory and clinical trial purposes.

title: Standardized endpoint definitions for transcatheter aortic valve implantation clinical trials: a consensus report from the Valve Academic Research Consortium
key_finding: The Valve Academic Research Consortium established standardized consensus definitions for important clinical endpoints in TAVI clinical trials, including mortality, myocardial infarction, stroke, bleeding, acute kidney injury, vascular complications, and prosthetic valve performance.
clinical_implications: These consensus endpoint definitions provide consistency across TAVI studies to facilitate evaluation of this catheter-based therapy and are useful for regulatory and clinical trial purposes.

title: Acute kidney injury following transcatheter aortic valve implantation: predictive factors, prognostic value, and comparison with surgical aortic valve replacement
key_finding: Acute kidney injury occurred in 11.7% of patients following TAVI and was associated with a four-fold increase in postoperative mortality risk. In patients with pre-procedural chronic kidney disease, TAVI was associated with significantly lower incidence of AKI compared to SAVR (9.2% vs 25.9%).
clinical_implications: TAVI may be preferred over SAVR in patients with chronic kidney disease due to lower risk of acute kidney injury.

title: Percutaneous mitral valve repair with the MitraClip system: acute results from a real world setting
key_finding: MitraClip percutaneous edge-to-edge mitral valve repair achieved 96.8% acute device success with 93.6% 30-day safety endpoint freedom from major adverse events in 31 patients. A clip was successfully implanted in all patients with significant reduction in mitral regurgitation to ≤2+ in most cases.
clinical_implications: Results demonstrate that percutaneous mitral valve repair with MitraClip is feasible and safe with favorable short-term outcomes in real-world clinical practice.

title: Transcatheter valve implantation for patients with aortic stenosis: a position statement from the European Association of Cardio-Thoracic Surgery (EACTS) and the European Society of Cardiology (ESC), in collaboration with the European Association of Percutaneous Cardiovascular Interventions (EAPCI)
key_finding: This expert consensus position statement establishes that transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) is feasible and provides hemodynamic and clinical improvement for up to 2 years in high-risk patients with severe symptomatic aortic stenosis. The technique should be restricted to high-risk patients or those with contraindications for surgery at the time of publication.
clinical_implications: This consensus statement provided early guidance for the appropriate use of TAVI technology in high-risk aortic stenosis patients when the technique was in its early development phase.

title: Joint Surgical Associations (EACTS, LACES, ASCVTS, AATS and STS) position statement regarding the VARC-3 definitions for aortic valve clinical research
key_finding: Joint surgical associations provide recommendations on VARC-3 definitions for aortic valve research, highlighting concerns about re-hospitalization as primary endpoint, bleeding definitions, and valve thrombosis criteria in SAVR vs TAVI trials.
clinical_implications: This position statement aims to improve standardized outcome definitions for aortic valve intervention trials to ensure more accurate comparisons between SAVR and TAVI procedures.

title: Long-term Transcatheter Aortic Valve Durability: Data from the UK TAVI Registry
key_finding: Long-term transcatheter aortic valve function is excellent with 91% of patients remaining free of structural valve deterioration between 5-10 years post-implantation. Only 0.4% had severe structural valve degeneration and 8.7% had moderate structural valve degeneration.
clinical_implications: These findings support the durability of TAVI valves in the long-term, which is important as TAVI expands to lower-risk patient populations.

title: Five-year outcomes in trials comparing trans-catheter aortic valve implantation vs surgical aortic valve replacement: a pooled analysis of Kaplan-Meier derived individual patient data
key_finding: TAVI shows early survival advantage in first 24 months compared to SAVR, but this advantage reverses after 24 months with TAVI becoming associated with higher mortality and composite outcomes favoring SAVR. TAVI was also associated with increased rehospitalization after 1 year.
clinical_implications: The time-varying effects suggest TAVI's early benefits diminish over time, with SAVR showing superior long-term outcomes after 2 years, which is critical for treatment selection especially in younger, lower-risk patients.

title: Adaptations to Coronary Physiology in a Patient with Severe Aortic Stenosis and Complete Heart Block Undergoing Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement
key_finding: Complete heart block during TAVR was well tolerated due to rapid compensatory microcirculatory changes that preserved myocardial blood flow despite reduced mean aortic pressure. Right ventricular pacing resulted in less favorable coronary hemodynamics compared to complete heart block.
clinical_implications: Transient complete heart block episodes during TAVR may be better tolerated than previously thought, and right ventricular pacing may not improve coronary hemodynamic status at similar ventricular rates.

title: A Clinical Trial of Minothoracotomy versus Conventional Sternotomy for Mitral Valve Repair
key_finding: Minimally invasive mitral valve repair is not superior to conventional sternotomy in recovery of physical function at 12 weeks, but achieves similar high rates of valve repair (96%) and similar safety outcomes at 1 year.
clinical_implications: These findings can inform shared decision-making discussions with patients being evaluated for mitral valve repair surgery, as both approaches are equally safe and effective.
